replies.author: @rbdogwood
replies.text: I don't think this was aimed at a fashion conscious market and, more to the point, why did you watch it?

author: @andybaker2443
text: Nice one Luke. I found a couple of these as a kid (a long time ago). When you looked at the inside you could see the date and other text of the original coins.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: It is possible to do this, it just takes practice lol. Thanks mate!

author: @MichielvanderMeulen
text: strange video but you're handsome ;)

author: @rodciferri9626
text: Careful doing this in the US, for some reason I think it may be a crime - of course, I can't imagine anyone actually being arrested for it - lol!

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Nice and cool, it is however illegal to deface government minted tender. Just a coin I know but just saying how petty the letter of the law is.

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: This is not considered defacing the coin, at least not in the US. If you melt it down grind it up, dissolve it in acid or change its appearance so it no longer looks like a coin you are not breaking the law.

author: @emilee172
text: right well this kid IS annoying , just a little bit

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hiya mate - I don't really comment on Luke's videos and though I appreciate what he does or how he does it may not be to your taste - however - I do ask you try to remember that on this channel we try to have a positive approach to comments -you might have mentioned why you find it annoying to give Luke a chance to learn and improve rather than just voice an opinion - cheers and hoping you take this in the spirit it is meant

replies.author: @AM-us7cz
replies.text: Wrong attitude emilee, the boy dun good. Remember one mans trash is another mans treasure.

author: @OldMysticFantasist
text: Nicely done, Mate.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thank you mate. :)

author: @NorthernKitty
text: Fantastic job, and it's fun to watch you progress in making instructional videos. 😊  You're getting much more comfortable and at ease!!  It's not always easy to maintain enthusiasm without it seeming forced.  When I was learning to do public speaking, a mentor taught me a nifty trick to be both energetic and sincere:  Imagine that your very best friend is in the audience and you're sharing with them a topic you're both excited about.  Worked like a charm every time!!   Watching this video reminded me of that, as I really felt you were genuinely enthused to share this with us, like we're your best friend.  🥰  I see a very bright future for you, Luke, keep up the good work!!

author: @ronaldd2154
text:
(In Elvish) : ash nazg thrakatulûk agh burzum-ishi krimpatul!

Translated into English: 

"One Ring to rule them all, One Ring to find them, One Ring to bring them all, and in the darkness bind them!." 💍 😂 well done.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hahahaha awesome! I love lord of the rings. Thanks mate! :)

author: @herbetone
text: I first made one of these fifty years ago, I used half crowns back in the day . Its great to see all the lettering turn round to the inside. Thanks for invoking some of my fond memories...Happy Dayz

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That is awesome, Im glad you liked the video! Thanks mate. :)

author: @Nick_Tag
text: Now i know what to do with the couple of old £1 coins i’m yet to trade in at the bank, or is that not legal lol

replies.author: @ronaldd2154
replies.text: Yes melt it down into bullion bars, or open up a "ring" business on ebay now 100% recycled/good for planet "Purity rings" 💍💍💍 😂

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hahaha I have not seen one of them in a while now! lol.

replies.author: @rbdogwood
replies.text: Thanks, I thought it was no longer possible to cash them in. You've just saved me £7.

author: @AmericanQRoy
text: People make rings out of coins because they want to see the coin markings. We could use all kinds of material to make a ring that doesn’t show any markings

author: @johndii2194
text: In 1970 we held the coin with our fingers and hit it with an spoon.

replies.author: @ronbean1628
replies.text:
You beat me to the draw. Bore a hole in the middle and spin it on a toothpick with a spoon to beat it
This was outlawed at school because of the dinging

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text: When I was a lad we used to take our groats to the local blacksmith and... Oops, I think I'm showing my age!

author: @johang1293
text: Just beware that in some countries it's illegal to alters, defaces, mutilates, impairs, diminishes, falsifies, scales, or lightens coins  and currency. Check your local laws before doing this at home.

replies.author: @SlyerFox666
replies.text: Yea it's law in the UK, but then again you can go Alton towers an put a penny in a machine an get it stamped an other places. So it's just another bs law that's not enforced, and if it was then there are plenty of places you could say do it and charge legal tender to do so, making a profit from breaking the law, which you are not, if you are using your own money to do so and not selling them. 😉

replies.author: @johang1293
replies.text: @SlyerFox666 Those machines are made to look like they use a coin for the press but they actually use a different piece of metal.

replies.author: @ronaldd2154
replies.text:
Lol was just about to advocate this too!

In America, I remember people melting down those old coins trying to convert it into scrap metal / bullion bars as they'd make a slight profit now by weight instead 😂.

replies.author: @jamescunningham6017
replies.text: Only if its in circulation.

replies.author: @SlyerFox666
replies.text: @johang1293 No it's the coin you put in you get back. We used an old shilling and got back an old shilling, no switcheroo. Not saying there isn't such a machine but it's not the ones I'm talking about.
